planting.note: Plant container-grown or balled-and-burlapped specimens in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; white fringetree establishes slowly but is long-lived and exceptionally adaptable once rooted in. It is dioecious — female plants produce ornamental blue-black fruits attractive to wildlife if a male is nearby, though both sexes flower showily. Seed requires warm stratification followed by cold stratification and may take 18 months to germinate; nursery-grown transplants are strongly preferred.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil; white fringetree is one of the most spectacular native flowering trees of the eastern United States, erupting in late April and May in airy, drooping panicles of pure-white, strap-like petals that give the effect of shimmering white fringe covering the entire canopy — often before or just as the leaves emerge, which amplifies the floral display dramatically. Chionanthus virginicus is dioecious: female plants produce loose clusters of olive-sized, blue-black, grape-like fruits in late summer that are highly attractive to birds, particularly thrushes, mockingbirds, and waxwings; male plants typically produce slightly showier and more fragrant flower clusters. Exceptionally adaptable to a wide range of soil types including clay, loam, and sandy soils, and tolerant of urban conditions, air pollution, and occasional flooding once established — making it an outstanding choice for residential landscapes, streetscapes, and naturalistic borders throughout zones 3–9. Grows slowly at 6–10 inches per year; patience yields a magnificent specimen that can live for decades with virtually no maintenance.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — NC State University Plants Database

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in fall or early spring in moist to consistently moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; California spicebush is native to riparian streambanks and moist canyon slopes of the California Coast Ranges and Sierra Nevada foothills and needs reliable soil moisture to thrive. In drier sites, supplemental irrigation through summer is essential. Seed benefits from 3 months of cold moist stratification before sowing.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil with consistent moisture; California spicebush is native to riparian corridors, streambanks, and canyon bottoms in California and performs best where roots can access consistent moisture — it is more drought-sensitive than its eastern relative Calycanthus floridus and should not be allowed to dry out completely during summer. Calycanthus occidentalis produces large, uniquely structured flowers from late spring through summer — burgundy-red to maroon, multi-petaled blooms with a warm, spicy-sweet fragrance resembling red wine and cloves, making it a standout sensory plant in woodland gardens and shaded borders. The bold, rough-textured, aromatic leaves turn yellow in fall. An excellent large-scale native shrub for shaded rain gardens, streamside plantings, and California native gardens in zones 6–9; pairs beautifully with native ferns, Ribes species, and Cercis occidentalis.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained to evenly moist soil in partial shade to full shade; Henry's anise is native to moist woodland ravines and shaded slopes of central China and demands protection from hot afternoon sun in zones 7–9 or foliage will scorch. Avoid low frost pockets where cold air pools; a sheltered courtyard or north-facing slope provides ideal conditions in the colder end of its range.

directions: Plant in partial shade to deep shade in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil with excellent drainage; Henry's anise is one of the finest shade-tolerant broad-leaved evergreen shrubs for gardens in zones 7–9, offering year-round glossy, lance-shaped, deep-green foliage with a distinct anise fragrance when crushed — a characteristic shared with the closely related native Florida anise (Illicium floridanum). Illicium henryi produces charming, star-shaped flowers from spring through early summer in shades of rose-red to deep carmine-pink, with petals arranged in the distinctive multi-layered pattern characteristic of the genus; the flowers are subtly fragrant and appear among the glossy foliage over an extended season. An outstanding plant for shady courtyards, woodland borders, and foundation plantings where a refined, deer-resistant, evergreen structure is needed; it pairs beautifully with Japanese maples, camellias, and Hydrangea quercifolia in the shade garden. CAUTION: all parts are toxic if ingested — do not confuse with culinary star anise (Illicium verum).
sources: North Carolina State Extension — NC State University Plants Database

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in moist, acidic, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Japanese snowbell performs best with protection from hot afternoon sun in zones 7–8 and benefits from a sheltered position that protects flowers from late frosts, which can damage emerging buds. Amend heavy clay soils generously with compost to ensure good drainage and aeration. Seed requires 3 months of cold moist stratification and germinates slowly; nursery transplants are strongly recommended.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Japanese snowbell is one of the most elegant and refined small ornamental trees for the garden, producing in late May and June an extraordinary display of pendant, pure-white, bell-shaped flowers hanging in rows beneath the horizontal branching — creating a layered, wedding-cake silhouette when viewed from below that reveals the full beauty of its blooms. Styrax japonicus is a graceful multi-stemmed or lightly branched small tree with excellent horizontal structure and clean, fine-textured dark-green foliage that turns yellow to red in fall; the cultivars 'Pink Chimes' and 'Carillon' offer weeping forms and pink-flushed flowers for smaller gardens. It performs particularly well as an understory tree beneath high-canopy oaks, planted where afternoon shade protects it from drought stress — it is intolerant of drought, alkaline soils, and compacted sites. Virtually pest- and disease-free, making it an outstanding choice for zones 5–8 gardens seeking a refined, low-maintenance flowering tree of exceptional beauty.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — NC State University Plants Database

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; sinojackia is a rare Chinese woodland tree that establishes best in a sheltered site with protection from desiccating winds and hot afternoon sun, especially in zones 7–9. It is rarely available from mainstream nurseries — seek out specialty native and rare woody plant nurseries or botanical garden plant sales. Soil pH should be kept between 5.5 and 6.5 for best performance.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil; sinojackia is one of the rarest and most underused small ornamental trees in cultivation — a member of the Styracaceae family (relatives include Styrax and Halesia), it produces profuse, pure-white, star-shaped flowers with prominent yellow stamens in spring, blooming along the branches as the new leaves emerge and creating an effect reminiscent of a refined, smaller-scale Halesia carolina. Sinojackia xylocarpa is native to a restricted area of eastern China and is considered endangered in its native range, making cultivated plants in gardens an important contribution to ex-situ conservation. The flowers are followed by distinctive woody, beaked fruits that persist into winter and provide additional ornamental interest; the multi-stemmed habit and clean, oval leaves offer good fall color. An exceptional conversation piece for collectors and enthusiasts, best displayed as a specimen or focal point in a woodland garden where its delicate flowers can be appreciated at close range.
